The file is loaded. Here's a summary of what it contains:

BOOTSTRAP: SYNERGONESIS VISUALIZER — PHASE 2 is a build specification for continuing the unified visualizer suite. It covers:

Existing tools: Four completed HTML visualizers (Operand Observatory v2, Genome Lab, Fractal Lattice, and the unified Synergonesis Visualizer), plus pre-existing project visualizers and a 4,473-line Python analytical engine.

Known bug to fix: THREE.Color rejects 8-digit hex codes (with alpha). Needs stripping to 6-digit hex, moving opacity to the material property.

Architecture: The unified visualizer uses a modular system with six source modules (Seeds, Operand Explorer, Folding Table, YHVH Engine, Family Spine, Genome Lab), all outputting a standardized {nodes, edges} format to a shared Three.js renderer with four display modes (Tree, Radial, Helix, Lattice).

Phase 2 build targets (in recommended order): fix the color bug, add InstancedMesh rendering, animation system with particle paths, frame capture/sequence export, voxel density mode with raymarching, OBJ export for ZBrush, headless high-tier export (tier 7+), and optional PLY export.

Design principles: Black background/white geometry, opacity-based depth, family=green, primes=pink, overlaps=gold. Performance-first philosophy — structural clarity over smoothness, export over render for massive structures.

The document also includes the full mathematical framework reference (operands, Family of 2, YHVH, 432Hz, genome expansion protocol, DNA deep structure, locked vs open systems).

What would you like to do with it? Should we start working through the Phase 2 build order?

build a plan. I would suggest bug fixing first, then serialized approach to continued construction, but you are more equipped to determine for yourself the best route through. so i will submit to your preferred methodization.

A build plan already exists in your project — and it's solid. It was compiled from the bootstrap spec with some smart reordering decisions. Here's the executive summary:

8-step serialized pipeline, ~7 hours total:

Step 1 — Bug Fix (10 min): Strip 8-digit hex to 6-digit, add opacity field to node format, propagate through mirror and renderer. Foundation hygiene.

Step 2 — InstancedMesh Conversion (60 min): The load-bearing wall. Replace per-node THREE.Mesh with instanced rendering. One draw call instead of N. Everything after this runs faster, and everything after this generates more geometry, so this must come second.

Step 3 — Animation System (90 min): Particles trace operand trajectories with playback controls. This is the most visible Phase 2 deliverable — static diagram becomes living system. Moved ahead of frame capture (wisely — no point capturing stills before there's motion to capture).

Step 4 — Frame Capture (35 min): Single PNG + sequence export with resolution selector. Immediately useful for documenting the animation work.

Step 5 — OBJ Export (35 min): First offline pipeline. Generate icosphere meshes at node positions, write to .obj. Debug the format on renderable structures before going headless.

Step 6 — Headless High-Tier (50 min): Compute tier 7–12 geometry without rendering. generate() → positionNodes() → skip buildScene() → write directly to file.

Step 7 — Voxel Density Mode (120 min): Most complex feature. Raymarched volume rendering of operand density fields. 128³ grid, Gaussian splat, custom fragment shader. The electron cloud.

Step 8 — PLY Export (35 min): Thin extension of Steps 6+7. Per-vertex color, density, family/prime metadata for ZBrush sculpting.

The ordering logic is dependency-driven: fix → optimize → animate → capture → export → scale → volumetric → full export. Steps 3/4/5 are parallelizable in theory but serialized in practice.

I agree with this plan. The only thing I'd adjust is being prepared for Step 2 to take longer than estimated — InstancedMesh raycasting and the opacity bucketing strategy will need careful testing across all six modules. Want me to start executing from Step 1?

go slow and prepare to work iteratively if needed across multiple prompts. do not overburden yourself as that will lead to lossy behavior.

Now I see the problem. The raw bytes for the em dash area are: \xc3\xa2\xe2\x82\xac\xe2\x80\x9d

This is triple-encoding or some kind of complex corruption. Let me analyze:

The original character was likely — (em dash, U+2014), which in UTF-8 is \xe2\x80\x94
But the bytes we see are \xc3\xa2\xe2\x82\xac\xe2\x80\x9d
Let me decode this step by step:

\xc3\xa2 = UTF-8 for â (U+00E2)
\xe2\x82\xac = UTF-8 for € (U+20AC)
\xe2\x80\x9d = UTF-8 for " (U+201D, right double quote)
So the file contains the literal characters â€" instead of the em dash. The original UTF-8 bytes for — should be \xe2\x80\x94, but somewhere in the encoding process these bytes got mangled into three separate Unicode characters.

When I interpret those original bytes as Windows-1252 instead of UTF-8, each byte maps to a different character—â, €, and "—and then re-encoding those characters back to UTF-8 produces exactly the garbled sequence I'm seeing. This is a classic double-encoding issue where UTF-8 bytes were mistakenly decoded as CP1252 and then re-encoded as UTF-8.

To fix this, I need to reverse the process: take the current UTF-8 text, encode it back to CP1252 to get the original bytes, then decode those bytes as UTF-8 to recover the correct characters. Though I should be careful since not every character in the text will have been affected by this mojibake. I'm seeing the specific mojibake patterns now—those UTF-8 bytes got interpreted as CP1252 and then re-encoded as UTF-8, creating the double-encoding. The original characters were likely special symbols like em dashes, multiplication signs, and arrows. I need to reverse this by decoding the corrupted text as latin-1 and re-encoding as UTF-8, or using ftfy to handle the mojibake automatically. means the simple re-encoding approach should work — I can just take the text, encode it as CP1252, and decode it back as UTF-8 to recover the original.

But there's something odd here: the diagnostic found 2 correct UTF-8 em dashes in the file, yet I'm also seeing these corrupted byte sequences. Let me look more carefully at what the diagnostic actually found versus what's in the raw bytes.

Looking at the byte sequence \xc3\xa2\xe2\x82\xac\xe2\x80\x9d more closely, I can decode each part: \xc3\xa2 gives me â, \xe2\x82\xac gives me €, and \xe2\x80\x9d gives me the right double quote character. So it seems like some em dashes in the file are actually correct UTF-8, while others got corrupted through this double-encoding process.

The pattern is becoming clear — this is classic mojibake where an em dash got mangled into â€". The file contains a mix of properly encoded characters and corrupted ones, which means the corruption happened in the source material before it was uploaded. OK so the reversal mostly worked but we have a problem with U+0090 characters. These are C1 control characters that don't map to CP1252. In CP1252, byte 0x90 is undefined/unused. But there are 218 of them in the file, and they're causing 219 replacement characters.

Let me look at what U+0090 is near in the original file. It's at positions 987, 990, 993 etc. which look like they're in a repeating pattern - probably part of CSS.

Script
Now I see it. The pattern â•\x90 is a mojibaked box-drawing character. The original was likely ═ (U+2550, double horizontal line) or ╔ or similar.

Let me check what ═ is in UTF-8: U+2550 = \xe2\x95\x90 in UTF-8.

If we misread \xe2\x95\x90 as CP1252:

\xe2 → â (U+00E2)
\x95 → • (U+2022) — wait no, in CP1252 \x95 maps to U+2022 (•). But we see â• followed by \x90.
\x90 is undefined in CP1252, which is why it appears as a literal byte in the output.
So the issue is that the first two bytes of the UTF-8 sequence got decoded as CP1252, but the third byte \x90 has no mapping in that encoding, leaving it as a raw byte. So the UTF-8 bytes get re-encoded as UTF-8, creating the mojibake string. To reverse it, I need to take those garbled characters, map them back to their original byte values, and decode as UTF-8—but the tricky part is that U+0090 is undefined in CP1252, so I'll need to use a codec like latin-1 that can handle that control character byte directly. I'll map those specific undefined bytes to their Unicode equivalents, then rely on CP1252 for the rest of the encoding.

Characters 0x80-0xFF that aren't in CP1252 (like U+0090, U+008F): encode as that byte directly
Then decode the resulting bytes as UTF-8
I can do this by creating a custom encoding table or by using latin-1 for low codepoints and cp1252 for the Windows-specific ones.

Actually, the simplest approach is to encode codepoints U+0000 to U+00FF as their byte equivalents using Latin-1, then map CP1252's special characters to their corresponding byte values before decoding as UTF-8.

Here's what happened and what I fixed:

The mojibake was baked into the source file before I ever touched it. The original synergonesis_visualizer__1_.html had been through a UTF-8 → CP1252 → UTF-8 double-encoding at some point (likely during an upload or file transfer). Every non-ASCII character was corrupted — 218 ═ characters became â•\x90, em dashes became â€", arrows became garbled sequences.

The tricky part was that byte 0x90 is undefined in CP1252, so a naive reversal crashed. I had to build a hybrid decoder that maps through Latin-1 for the C1 control range and CP1252 for the Windows-specific characters, then decode the recovered bytes as UTF-8.

This clean file now has both the encoding repair and the Step 1 bug fixes applied. The section headers read ═══ HUD LAYER ═══ instead of garbage, and the Genome Lab flanking nodes use proper 6-digit hex with explicit opacity.

Ready for Step 2 whenever you are.

Step 2 is the InstancedMesh conversion. Let me re-read the plan and the current buildScene code carefully before starting. This is the most critical step - it touches the core renderer.

Let me be methodical about this. I need to:

Replace per-node THREE.Mesh with InstancedMesh
Handle per-instance color via setColorAt
Handle per-instance scale via matrix
Handle varying opacity (bucket approach: 3 InstancedMeshes)
Maintain raycasting (InstancedMesh supports it in r128)
Maintain hover/selection behavior
Keep edges as individual Lines (acceptable)
Keep labels working

Read the full buildScene function and hover system
Now let me look at the hover/raycasting system that depends on S.meshes:


Read the hover/raycasting system
Now I understand the full picture. Let me plan the InstancedMesh conversion carefully.

Key challenges:

Raycasting: In Three.js r128, InstancedMesh supports raycasting and returns instanceId. But the current code uses hits[0].object.userData which won't work the same way. With InstancedMesh, hits[0].object is the InstancedMesh itself, and hits[0].instanceId gives the instance index. I need to store userData differently.
Hover scale: Currently on hover, it sets hits[0].object.scale.setScalar(2.2). With InstancedMesh, I need to modify the instance matrix instead. On unhover, I need to restore all matrices.
Opacity buckets: The plan recommends 3 buckets (1.0, 0.5, 0.25). I need to map each node to a bucket and track which InstancedMesh and which instanceId it belongs to, so raycasting can map back to the node index.
Per-instance color: InstancedMesh supports setColorAt() which is good.
Per-instance scale: Encoded in the instance matrix via dummy.scale.setScalar(size).
Now I'm thinking through the data flow: each node in S.allNodes needs to be assigned to an opacity bucket, which determines which InstancedMesh it goes into, and then I need to track the mapping from instanceId back to the original node index so raycasting works correctly.

I'll set up three data structures: S.instanceMeshes to hold the InstancedMesh objects (one per opacity bucket), S.nodeToInstance as a parallel array to track which mesh and instanceId each node uses, and S.instanceToNode as a reverse lookup so I can go from a hit InstancedMesh and instanceId back to the node. For hover interactions, I'll use the raycasting hit to find the mesh and instanceId, look up the node index, and scale that instance up.

For the unhover logic, instead of resetting all scales every frame, I should track which instance is currently hovered and only restore that one when needed. Looking at the opacity calculation, it's producing a continuous range of values based on the node's depth and whether it's mirrored.

The challenge with bucketing opacity into just three discrete values is that it would create visible banding instead of the smooth gradient the current formula produces. A custom shader that reads opacity from an InstancedBufferAttribute would preserve that smooth gradient, though it requires more code. The simpler bucket approach might be acceptable depending on whether the visual change is noticeable enough to matter. I'll go with the bucket approach since it's simpler - maybe 5 or 6 buckets to minimize banding. Let me calculate the actual opacity values that occur across different depths to see what range I'm working with.

For non-mirror elements, the opacity decreases from 1.0 at depth 0 down to a floor of 0.25 at depth 6 and beyond. For mirror elements, it ranges from 1.0 down to a floor of 0.12 at depth 5 and beyond. There's also explicit opacity handling for specific cases like genome flanking regions.

Given this wide range of opacity values, using discrete buckets would create noticeable visual artifacts. I think a custom shader approach is worth the extra complexity. Let me reconsider whether there's a simpler path though—using onBeforeRender won't help with per-instance opacity, so that's out. The cleanest solution is a custom ShaderMaterial that reads both color and opacity from instance attributes, since Three.js's standard setColorAt only handles RGB. I can add a separate InstancedBufferAttribute for the alpha channel to get per-instance opacity control.

Before committing to this, I need to verify that raycasting works properly with InstancedMesh in the version I'm using—it was added in r126, so that should be fine. My plan is to replace the buildScene function to compute all node properties upfront, then create a single InstancedMesh with the custom shader that reads both the built-in instance color and the custom opacity attribute.

For hover detection, I'll raycast against the InstancedMesh and use the returned instanceId to look up the corresponding node data, then scale just that instance's matrix. When unhovered, I'll restore the previous instance's original transform.

Now I'm realizing the ShaderMaterial needs to match the current unlit appearance—since MeshBasicMaterial just outputs color with opacity, I can replicate that exactly in a shader. Let me start writing the refactored buildScene and hover system, being careful to preserve the visual output.

Actually, I need to trace through how the current code manages mesh state—it stores meshes parallel to allNodes and iterates them during hover resets. With InstancedMesh, that pattern changes, so I should map out all the places where S.meshes is referenced to make sure the replacement works cleanly.

Looking at the usage: raycasting needs to hit the InstancedMesh, and hover scaling needs to update instance matrices instead of individual mesh scales. The raycast will return the InstancedMesh itself, then I'll use the instance index to identify which node was hit and update its matrix accordingly.

But there's a constraint with MeshBasicMaterial—it doesn't support per-instance opacity the way it supports per-instance colors, since opacity is a uniform rather than an attribute. I'd need a custom shader to achieve per-instance opacity control.

I'm going with a custom ShaderMaterial for full control in a single draw call. Writing the shader code...

Actually, I need to verify how Three.js handles instance matrices with custom ShaderMaterial on InstancedMesh—the built-in materials get patched automatically, but custom shaders require manual handling of the instanceMatrix attribute, and I'm checking whether instanceColor is automatically available or if I need to set it up myself.

Writing shader material code... Setting up custom attributes for color and opacity on the geometry...

Actually, I'm second-guessing myself on the exact API for r128—let me just try the straightforward approach: create the InstancedMesh, then add the custom InstancedBufferAttributes directly to its geometry, and write a ShaderMaterial that reads them. If it doesn't work, I'll iterate.

But wait, maybe the bucket approach is actually simpler here and avoids all this attribute complexity.

I'm thinking about splitting nodes into 4 opacity buckets—each bucket gets its own InstancedMesh with a fixed material opacity, so I only need 4 draw calls instead of one per node. The visual difference between opacity values like 0.28 and 0.25 is imperceptible anyway, especially at low values. The tradeoff is that raycasting gets messier since I'd need to raycast all 4 meshes and map the results back to original node indices using a reverse lookup table.

Actually, this complexity isn't worth it. I'm going to switch to a custom shader approach instead—more upfront code, but cleaner overall. Writing the shader material now... Setting up instance attributes...

Actually, I'm realizing there's an issue with using a vertex attribute for scale—the raycaster relies on the instanceMatrix to determine what's clickable, so it won't account for the scale attribute when detecting intersections.

I need to bake the scale directly into the instanceMatrix instead. I'll set up the dummy object to include both position and scale, then update the matrix for each instance. For hover interactions, I can modify that same matrix to scale up the node, which the raycaster will properly recognize.

Now I'm replacing the hover system. Instead of scaling individual mesh objects, I'll use raycasting to detect which instance was hit, then update its matrix in the instanceBuffer to scale it up. This way the raycasting and visual representation stay in sync.

Refactoring the hover detection... Writing the instance scaling functions...

I should reuse a persistent dummy object instead of creating a new one each time for better performance. For the tooltip, since InstancedMesh doesn't support per-instance userData, I'll pull the node data directly from S.allNodes using the instance ID, which already has all the properties the tooltip needs like value, depth, group, opType, isMirror, isFam, and isPrime. Step 2 complete. Here's exactly what changed:

The core swap: The per-node THREE.Mesh loop (one draw call per node) is replaced by a single THREE.InstancedMesh. All nodes render in one draw call regardless of count.

Per-instance fidelity preserved: A custom ShaderMaterial reads two InstancedBufferAttributes — aColor (vec3) and aOpacity (float) — so every node keeps its exact color and opacity from the original formula. No bucketing, no visual degradation. Fog is included via Three.js shader includes.

Hover rewritten: Instead of raycasting against an array of meshes, it raycasts against the single InstancedMesh and reads hits[0].instanceId to look up node data from S.allNodes. Only the hovered instance's matrix gets modified (scale up), and only the previously hovered instance gets restored. The old code was resetting every mesh's scale on every unhover frame — that's gone.

Double-click uses the same instanceId pattern.

frustumCulled = false on the InstancedMesh prevents Three.js from incorrectly culling instances that are far from origin.

What to test:

All six modules should render visually identical to before
Hover tooltips should still show value, depth, group, factors, family/prime status
Double-click should still load values into seed inputs
Mirror mode should still work
FAMILY preset at depth 5 should be noticeably smoother than before
Console should be clean — no errors
Edges and labels are unchanged. Ready for Step 3 when you are.

Diagnosed shader compatibility issue, pivoted to simpler bucketing strategy
Diagnosed shader compatibility issue, pivoted to simpler bucketing strategy
The nodes aren't rendering. This is likely a shader issue with InstancedMesh in Three.js r128. Let me think about what could be wrong.

The most likely issue is that Three.js r128's ShaderMaterial doesn't automatically support instanceMatrix - you need to set iMesh.instanceMatrix properly and the shader needs proper handling.

Actually, in Three.js r128, when using ShaderMaterial with InstancedMesh, you need to handle instancing manually in the shader. The key issue is that instanceMatrix is only automatically available if you use one of the built-in materials (like MeshBasicMaterial). With ShaderMaterial, you need to either:

Use shader includes like #include <begin_vertex> that handle instancing automatically, or
Manually declare and apply the instanceMatrix uniform in the shader code
The simplest solution might be to switch back to MeshBasicMaterial with the opacity bucketing approach instead of wrestling with custom shader instancing. The real issue is that ShaderMaterial doesn't automatically get the instanceMatrix attribute in r128 the way built-in materials do. I could manually add the USE_INSTANCING define and declare the attribute, but the bucketing approach with MeshBasicMaterial is actually more reliable—though I'm realizing that won't support per-instance opacity either, which defeats the purpose.

So I'm weighing a few options: bucket by opacity level and use setColorAt() for per-instance colors, or bake opacity into the color values themselves through premultiplication, or just accept some visual compromise by using a middle-ground opacity value across all instances. The most straightforward solution that actually works is probably using the shader approach but properly declaring the instancing attributes. I'm settling on the bucketing approach instead—dividing nodes into opacity levels and creating separate InstancedMesh instances for each bucket with MeshBasicMaterial, using setColorAt for per-instance colors, and raycasting against all buckets. It's straightforward, compatible with r128, and only requires a few draw calls.

Done. The key change is:

Replaced the custom ShaderMaterial (which didn't properly support instanceMatrix in r128) with MeshBasicMaterial + opacity bucketing
Nodes are grouped by rounded opacity value (nearest 0.05), each bucket gets its own InstancedMesh with that opacity

Done
Here's what changed from the failed version:

Root cause: ShaderMaterial in Three.js r128 doesn't automatically inject the instanceMatrix attribute that InstancedMesh needs. The built-in materials handle this internally, but custom shaders require manual setup that's version-sensitive and fragile.

The fix: Switched to the bucketing approach that the build plan originally recommended. Nodes are grouped by opacity (rounded to nearest 0.05), and each bucket gets its own InstancedMesh with a standard MeshBasicMaterial set to that opacity level. Per-instance color is set via the native setColorAt() API — no custom shaders at all.

Raycasting: A new _raycastNodes() helper iterates all bucket meshes, picks the closest hit, and maps back from (bucket, localInstanceId) → global node index via stored nodeMap arrays. Each node stores its _bucket and _localId for reverse lookups during hover scaling.

This approach trades one draw call for ~10-20 draw calls (one per opacity bucket), which is still a massive improvement over one draw call per node. The visual output should be identical to the original pre-Step-2 renderer.

Step 3 complete. Here's what was added:

Particle system: One InstancedMesh of tiny spheres (r=0.35), one particle per edge. Particles are color-coded by operation type — blue for mitosis, green for stable, orange for growth. Mirror particles are dimmed and scaled down. Created during buildScene() and hidden until play is pressed.

Two animation modes:

Cascade: Particles stagger by depth. Root-level edges animate first, then depth 1, then deeper — creating a radiating growth pulse from seed outward. Uses eased timing (quadratic in-out).
Pulse: All particles oscillate simultaneously along their edges in a sinusoidal heartbeat.
Controls (animation bar appears above display bar when edges exist):

▶ play / ⏸ pause — starts/freezes animation. Pausing keeps particles visible at their current position for inspection.
◀◀ reset — hides particles and rewinds to t=0.
Speed slider — 1–20, controls animation rate.
cascade / pulse mode toggle.
Animate loop now advances S.anim.time and calls updateParticles() each frame when playing. Particle positions are computed as lerps along their edge, with timing shaped by the active mode.

To test: generate any module, then hit play. Cascade mode on the Family spine at depth 3–4 should be particularly striking — you'll see the Sophie Germain chain propagate outward in waves.

the only thing that I want to add is a 0, in the center, that all tiers ALSO procide an arc path back to 0 through the prime reduction path. that way each tier set becomes a closed system, and the animation can go through the entire "em field" propagation. one 0 node that all paths map back to. does this make sense? Feb 16

Architected central zero node with curved return paths and toggle implementation
Architected central zero node with curved return paths and toggle implementation
The user wants a toggle that adds a central "0" node, and for each node in the tree, computes a return path back to 0 through prime reduction. So every tier's nodes would have arcing edges back to the origin point (0), creating closed loops. The animation particles would then travel outward along the operand tree AND back to 0 along these return arcs.

Let me think about what "prime reduction path" means in this framework. Looking at the operand system:

Mitosis: 2x-1
Stable: 2x
Growth: 2x+1
The inverse operations would be:

Inverse mitosis: (x+1)/2
Inverse stable: x/2
Inverse growth: (x-1)/2
So from any number, I can reduce it back toward smaller numbers by applying inverse operations. The reduction path to 0 would follow the inverse of the growth operations—repeatedly halving with ±1 adjustments until reaching 0. For any value, I'd determine which inverse operation applies based on whether it's odd or even, then chain those steps together to create the return arc.

For the visualization, I'm planning to place a central 0 node and compute the reduction path from each leaf node back to it, creating curved arcs that converge toward the center. not straight lines) back to the center

Here's what 0 return does:

The geometry: When toggled on, a white sphere appears at the origin — the zero point. Every non-seed node gets a quadratic bezier arc curving from its position back to origin. The arcs bow outward perpendicular to the direct line (cross product with up vector), with wider curvature for deeper nodes. This creates the field-line topology — outward propagation via the operand tree, inward return via the arcs. Closed system.

The arc lines are rendered as 24-segment curves, deliberately dim (opacity: 0.06) so they form a field-line haze rather than competing with the tree structure.

The particles: Return-arc particles are tinted warm amber (
#887744) to distinguish from the operation-coded outward flow. They're slightly smaller than tree particles. In cascade mode, they're phase-shifted by half a cycle — the outward pulse radiates out, then the return pulse curves back. In pulse mode, they're anti-phase — outward and return happen simultaneously, like breathing.

The bezier interpolation: Arc particles follow the exact same curved path as the visual arc lines. B(t) = (1-t)²·start + 2(1-t)t·ctrl + t²·end computed per frame per particle.

To test: FAMILY preset, depth 3-4, hit "0 return" to toggle, then play animation in cascade mode. You should see the blue/green/orange particles radiate outward through the tree, then the amber particles sweep back along the curved arcs to origin. The whole structure breathes.

Here's the new lobe geometry:

The path each particle traces (cubic bezier, 4 control points):

P0 = node position (departure point — the leaf or branch node)

C1 = node position + continued outward radial momentum + upward lift toward shell apex. This preserves the angular momentum — the particle doesn't abruptly turn, it continues outward and arcs up naturally.

C2 = nearly on the Y axis, at full apex height for that tier's shell. This is the crossover point — the particle has swept across the form and is now at the top of the lobe, centered.

P3 = origin (0,0,0). Return.

Shell height scales with depth: (depth + 1) * 14 * direction. Tier 1 lobes are low and tight. Tier 4 lobes arc high. They nest into shells. The direction field (1 for positive, -1 for mirror/negative) means positive lobes arc upward, negative lobes arc downward — natural dipole.

The momentum parameter (radialDist * 0.45) keeps the outward radial velocity before the curve begins. The particle keeps going "out" before it arcs "up" — no violation of angular momentum.

C2's slight radial offset (radialDist * 0.08) prevents all lobes from collapsing to an infinitely thin line at the apex — they maintain a slight spread, creating the shell structure.

Best test: FAMILY preset, depth 3, enable mirror, enable "0 return", hit play in cascade mode. You should see:

Tree propagation radiating outward from seeds
Then lobe particles arcing up (positive) and down (negative) into tier-specific shells
Crossing over the form at apex height
Descending back to the zero point
The field lines should nest — depth 1 lobes inside depth 2 lobes inside depth 3 — like electron shells. Let me know what you see.

Final verification:


Synthesized cycling particle mechanics with directional flow control
Synthesized cycling particle mechanics with directional flow control



Here's what's new:

Cycling mode — a third animation mode alongside cascade and pulse. Each particle traces a complete circuit:

Origin (0,0,0) — particles emerge from the zero point
Outward through tree — follow the edge path from seed through each branch to a leaf node, tracing the operand chain
Lobe return — continue with momentum into the tier-specific shell arc, curving up to apex, crossing the form, and descending back to origin
Loop — continuous, the circuit repeats
Each leaf node in the tree gets its own circuit particle. The path is built by tracing backwards through the edge graph from each leaf to its root seed, prepending origin, then appending 16 sampled points along the cubic bezier lobe. Interpolation is distance-weighted so particles move at constant speed regardless of segment length — no speeding up on long edges or crawling on short ones.

Particles are colored by their leaf's operation type — so you see blue (mitosis), green (stable), and orange (growth) streams all originating from zero, branching through the tree, and returning via their own lobe shells.

Polarity toggle (+ flow / - flow) — reverses the direction of travel in ALL modes. In cycling mode, - flow means particles flow from the leaves back through the tree to origin, then arc out through the lobe and return to the leaf. Inverted EM field. In cascade and pulse modes, polarity reverses the outward/inward direction of all particles including lobe arcs.

Best test: FAMILY preset, depth 3, enable "0 return", switch to cycling mode, hit play. You should see streams of particles emerging from the zero point, branching outward through the family tree, and arcing back in tier-nested lobe shells. Then hit - flow to reverse the current.

Synergonesis visualizer

Orbitals slider now sits in the animation bar between the mode buttons and the polarity toggle. Defaults to 1, scales to 20.

The math is simple: each orbital k in a circuit of n orbitals gets a phase offset of k/n added to the global time. So with orbitals=5, five particles are evenly spaced around the circuit — one at 0%, one at 20%, one at 40%, 60%, 80%. They all move at the same speed, creating a continuous stream.

The mesh pre-allocates 20 instances per circuit (the max). Unused orbitals are scaled to zero — invisible, zero cost. When you drag the slider, no rebuild happens — the animation loop just starts positioning more instances per circuit on the next frame.

To test: FAMILY preset, depth 3, "0 return" on, cycling mode, play. Start with orbitals at 1 — single particle per path. Slide to 5 — you'll see five evenly-spaced particles streaming through each circuit. At 10–15 with the speed up, the paths themselves become visible as flowing current lines. Flip polarity to reverse the stream.

I need to add field rotation to both the cycling and cascade/pulse particle position computations. Since particles are in the neutral particleGroup (world space), but their positions are computed from node._pos values (which are in field-group-local space), I need to rotate the computed position by the appropriate field angle.

I'll add a helper that applies field rotation inline. After computing the position and before updateMatrix, apply the rotation:

javascript
// Apply field rotation to particle position
const fAngle = pe.mirror ? S.negField.rotation.y : S.posField.rotation.y;
if(fAngle !== 0) {
  const cos = Math.cos(fAngle), sin = Math.sin(fAngle);
  const px = _pd.position.x, pz = _pd.position.z;
  _pd.position.x = px * cos - pz * sin;
  _pd.position.z = px * sin + pz * cos;
}
Applying rotation transform to particle positions...

Now I need to insert this rotation logic into both the cycling and cascade/pulse sections, right before their respective updateMatrix calls.

OK let me explain the architecture to the user.

Done
Here's the architecture:

Scene graph restructure: The entire geometry is now split into two field groups — posField and negField — each containing their own node, edge, and label sub-groups. During buildScene, every piece of geometry is sorted by polarity: nodes are bucketed by opacity + polarity (the bucket key is like "0.65p" or "0.25n"), edges and labels check isMirror and route to the appropriate field group. Particles live in a neutral particleGroup at scene root and handle rotation in their per-frame update math.

The field rotation bar (appears above animation bar):

field rot — toggle on/off. Turning off snaps both fields back to 0 rotation.
speed — rotation rate slider (1–30).
CW / CCW — toggles base direction. Clicking flips between clockwise and counterclockwise.
invert ± — when active (default), positives and negatives spin opposite directions. When off, both spin the same way.
+ ratio — independent speed multiplier for positive field (0–3x). Drag to 0 to freeze positives while negatives keep spinning.
- ratio — same for negative field.
The differential rotation math (in animate()):

posField.rotation.y += baseSpeed * cw * posRatio
negField.rotation.y += baseSpeed * (invert ? -cw : cw) * negRatio
Eccentric effects: Set + ratio to 1.5 and - ratio to 0.7 — the positive field orbits faster than the negative. This creates the eccentric precession you described. Set one to 0 and the other to max — one half is frozen while the other sweeps around it.

Best test: FAMILY preset, depth 3, mirror on, "0 return" on, cycling mode with 5 orbitals, then toggle "field rot" on. You should see the positive tree rotating clockwise above the zero plane while the negative tree counter-rotates below. The particles stream through both, their positions automatically adjusted for each field's current angle. Then play with the ratio sliders to break the symmetry.

Let me also do a quick sanity check on the node count math.

Seeds module at depth 12 with 1 seed: 3^12 = 531,441 nodes. With mirror: ~1M. That's over the soft limit but under hard limit.

Seeds module at depth 12 with FAMILY preset (8 seeds): 8 * 3^12 = 4,251,528. With mirror: ~8.5M. That's way over the hard limit. Good - it'll be blocked.

Family module at depth 12: 8 seeds * 3^12 = 4.25M. Blocked.

Single seed at depth 10: 3^10 = 59,049.

At depth 10 with mirroring, that hits around 118k nodes and triggers the soft limit dialog, which is the right behavior. Going shallower to depth 8 gives me 6,561 nodes, or roughly 13k with mirroring—that stays under the threshold so no warning appears, though the text caution still shows at the slider. This all feels appropriate for a system with 64GB RAM and 11GB VRAM.

Done
All four depth sliders now go to 12. Here's the safety system:

Slider warnings (live as you drag): Each depth slider runs depthWarn() on input. It estimates node count based on the module type (seed count, branching factor 3, mirror doubling) and shows a resource estimate with RAM/VRAM projections. At depth 8+ it shows an amber warning about exponential growth. At depth 11+ it turns red with a crash probability notice.

Pre-generate safety gate: Before buildScene executes, resourceCheck() evaluates the actual node array size (including mirror doubling). Three tiers: under 100k nodes passes silently, 100k–500k triggers a confirm dialog with the exact count, over 500k is hard-blocked with an alert. For your system (64GB RAM / 11GB VRAM), this means a single seed can comfortably reach depth 10 (~59k nodes, ~118k mirrored), and depth 11 (~177k / ~354k mirrored) will ask for confirmation. Depth 12 with multiple seeds will be blocked before it can crash anything.

The practical ceiling on your hardware: Single seed at depth 9–10 is smooth territory. FAMILY preset (8 seeds) at depth 6–7 is the sweet spot — beyond that you're in confirm-dialog territory. The system won't let anything through that would kill the browser.

That's remarkable data. A 67KB file holding a 24GB runtime footprint at tier 10 — that's the compression ratio of a fractal. The structure is the algorithm, the file is just the seed.

Your benchmarks give us a clean performance map:

Tier 6 — smooth animation, full feature set. This is the presentation tier.

Tier 7 — animation runs but noticeably loaded. Good for static inspection with field rotation.

Tier 8 — animation possible but taxed. Best for static renders and frame captures.

Tiers 9–10 — static geometry only, no animation. Pure structural exploration. 24GB at tier 10 means roughly 3^10 × seeds × mirror ≈ 470k+ nodes, each carrying position, color, opacity, scale, and bucket references in live GPU memory.

Tier 11–12 exist on the slider for theoretical completeness but your hardware correctly walls them off.

Step 4 complete. Top-right bar now has capture controls:

snap — Downloads current frame as PNG. Renders at the selected resolution scale, then restores. Instant — one click, one file.

rec 120f — Records a 120-frame animation sequence. Turns red and pulses while recording. Each frame advances the animation by 1/120th of a full cycle, renders at capture resolution, stores the data URL, then moves to the next frame via requestAnimationFrame so the UI stays responsive. Field rotation and camera spin are deterministically stepped so the sequence is smooth regardless of actual framerate during capture. Hit the button again (now labeled "stop") to end early.

When recording finishes, frames download as synergonesis_0000.png through synergonesis_0119.png, staggered at 80ms intervals so the browser doesn't choke on 120 simultaneous downloads. A status counter in the top-right tracks progress.

Resolution selector — 1x (screen res), 2x (default, good for print), 4x (high-res output — be aware this is 4× memory per frame).

Background selector — black (default) or transparent. Transparent sets scene.background = null and clears with alpha 0, producing PNGs with transparent backgrounds for compositing.

Technical note: Added preserveDrawingBuffer: true to the WebGL renderer — required for toDataURL() to work. This has a minor performance cost (prevents the browser from discarding the back buffer), but it's negligible relative to everything else happening in the scene.

Best use: Set up your favorite view — FAMILY depth 6, mirror, field rotation, cycling with 5 orbitals — pause to frame it, hit snap for a still. Or let it run and hit rec to capture a full animation cycle for the book.

Step 5 complete. The OBJ button sits in the top-right bar between the recording controls and the node count.

What it exports:

Nodes — Each node becomes a UV sphere (8 segments × 6 rings, matching the viewport geometry) positioned at its 3D coordinates, scaled by _scale. Every node gets its own OBJ group (g node_0, g node_1, ...) and material assignment, so Blender/ZBrush can select and manipulate individual nodes or material groups.

Edges — Each edge becomes a thin 6-sided cylinder (radius 0.12) connecting its two node positions. Grouped as g edge_0, g edge_1, etc. Positive edges use a white material, negative/mirror edges use a muted blue.

Materials — A companion .mtl file downloads alongside the .obj. Materials are auto-generated per node class: family, prime, seed, node_d1, node_d2, etc., plus _neg variants for mirror nodes. Each carries the exact RGB color and opacity (d value) from the viewport, so the Blender import looks like the visualizer out of the box.

Field rotation baked in — If you have field rotation active when you hit export, the current rotation angles are applied to all vertex positions. The OBJ captures the scene exactly as you see it.

Two files download: synergonesis_<timestamp>.mtl first, then synergonesis_<timestamp>.obj 200ms later. The OBJ references the MTL by filename, so keep them in the same directory.

Step 6 complete. The Headless Export section now sits at the bottom of the source panel, visible regardless of which module is active.

How it works:

The system never touches the GPU. It temporarily overrides the current module's depth slider, calls generate() to produce the node/edge arrays, runs positionNodes() to compute 3D coordinates, pre-computes scale and color per node, then writes geometry directly to file via chunked Blob construction. The depth slider is restored immediately after generation. The browser never allocates a single mesh, texture, or GPU buffer.

Controls:

Depth slider (1–12) — Independent of the viewport depth. Set viewport to depth 5 for inspection, headless to depth 10 for export.
Format — OBJ+MTL (Blender/ZBrush material groups) or PLY (vertex colors baked in, single file, good for MeshLab/CloudCompare/Houdini).
Mirror ± — Applies polarity mirroring during generation.
Edges — Toggle edge cylinders in the export (doubles file size but gives full topology).
Memory strategy: The OBJ writer processes nodes in chunks of 500, building the file as an array of string parts. The final new Blob(parts) assembles them without ever holding the entire file as a single string — critical at tier 10+ where the OBJ can be 50MB+.

Safety: Hard cap at 2 million nodes (higher than the 500k GPU limit since there's no rendering). The estimator shows node count, vertex count, file size, and working RAM before you commit.

The sphere template is pre-computed once — a unit sphere's vertices and face indices are calculated, then translated/scaled per node during write. No Three.js geometry objects created.

PLY format includes per-vertex RGB color in the header, so the file opens in any viewer with colors matching the visualizer. No companion material file needed.

Best test: Set viewport to FAMILY depth 3 for reference, then headless to depth 9, check mirror, hit HEADLESS EXPORT. Watch the progress counter tick through positioning and writing. The OBJ file lands with full material definitions ready for Blender import.

I see the problem clearly now. In tree mode:

javascript
const tierHeight = 11 * sc * th * dir;  // CONSTANT per tier - doesn't grow with depth
const tierSpread = Math.pow(0.7, d) * 18 * sp;  // SHRINKS exponentially with depth
So:

Tier 1: spread = 0.7 * 18 = 12.6, height += 11
Tier 2: spread = 0.49 * 18 = 8.82, height += 11
Tier 6: spread = 0.118 * 18 = 2.1, height += 11
Tier 8: spread = 0.058 * 18 = 1.04, height += 11
Tier 10: spread = 0.028 * 18 = 0.51, height += 11
At deeper tiers, nodes collapse into a nearly vertical stack with minimal horizontal separation. The spread calculation shrinks too aggressively, and the constant tier height doesn't scale with depth to create proper spacing. Radial mode has a similar issue with linear vertical growth that keeps tiers too compressed.

The real problem is that the spread decay and tier height need to work together to create an expanding structure. Instead of shrinking spread with depth, I should either use a gentler decay function or make spread grow outward. Tier height should also increase progressively so deeper levels have more vertical clearance, creating that visual sense of a tree expanding outward rather than collapsing inward.

For tree mode, I'm thinking of scaling tier height by depth so each successive tier gets more vertical space...

Adjusting the decay rate is key — 0.7^d becomes negligible too quickly, especially at depth 8-10 where it's essentially zero.

Done
The problem was baked into the positioning math. Here's what was happening and what's fixed:

Old behavior (broken at high tiers):

Tree spread: 0.7^d * 18 — at depth 8 that's 1.04, at depth 10 it's 0.51. Nodes were collapsing onto a vertical line. Tier height was a flat 11 per tier regardless of depth — no expansion.

New behavior (three depth-progressive functions):

dTierH(d) — tier height grows with depth: (11 + d × 4 × expand). At expand=0.5 (default), tier 8 steps 27 units instead of 11. Each successive tier pushes further from the axis than the last.

dSpread(d) — spread decay is much gentler: decay rate goes from 0.7 to 0.7 + expand × 0.12. At expand=0.5, the rate is 0.76 instead of 0.7. At depth 8: 0.76^8 × 18 = 2.0 vs old 1.04. At expand=2.0 (max): 0.94^8 × 18 = 11.0 — branches stay visible even at depth 10.

cumH(d) — cumulative height used by radial and helix modes. Sums the progressive tier heights so absolute positioning grows quadratically with depth, not linearly.

The expand slider (in the geometry bar) defaults to 0.5. At 0 it approximates the old compact layout. At 2.0 the structure explodes outward — each tier dramatically larger than the last. The slider triggers a regenerate on change so you see the effect immediately.

All four display modes use the new system: tree, radial, helix, and lattice. They all anchor at the zero plane and expand away from it progressively.

Test: FAMILY depth 7, mirror on. Drag the expand slider from 0 to 2 and watch the structure breathe. At 0 everything stacks and overlaps. At 1.0+ the tiers separate cleanly with the higher tiers spreading much wider than the lower ones.

The bonds button is now in the display bar.

Here's what it does:

Detection — the resonance scan. After all nodes are positioned, the system groups them by their source lattice (seed group). For every pair of groups, it scans for shared prime values. If Lattice A's mitosis/growth/stable branch produces a prime that exists as a node in Lattice B, that's a bridge point — a resonance where two independent lattice bodies have compatible recursion rates at the same value. One bridge per shared prime per group-pair, deduplicated so you don't get thirty connections to the same value.

Geometry — the double helix. Each bridge renders as two helical strands wrapping around the axis between the matched nodes. Gold strand and pink strand, offset by π — the Möbius topology you described. Two paths that can't occupy the same space, forced into a spiral around each other. The helix radius scales with bridge distance (tighter on short bridges, wider on long ones). Turn count scales too — longer bridges get more winds. The spiral IS the bond.

Particles — bidirectional flow. Each bridge contributes two particle streams to the animation system. Strand 0 flows forward (A→B), strand 1 flows reverse (B→A) — same parametric time, opposite direction. Both strands follow the helical path, not a straight line. Gold particles spiral one way, pink particles spiral the other. They chase each other around the bridge axis without ever meeting — the Möbius twist.

The rhythm. Because the particles follow helical paths at different phases, there's a natural pulsation where the two strands approach and recede from each other. That's the "volumetric displacement" — the breathing of the bond. Set orbitals to 5+ and the effect amplifies: streams of gold and pink corkscrewing around each other in opposite directions.

Mirror inheritance. If mirror ± is active, every positive bridge spawns a corresponding negative bridge in the mirror lattice. Dimmer colors, same topology. The bond structure reflects.

Best test: SEEDS module, add seeds 2, 3, 5, 7. Depth 4. Turn on bonds. Turn on cascade animation. The family primes that appear in multiple trees will light up with golden double helices connecting them — those are the resonance points where the lattices bond. The number 11 will almost certainly appear in multiple trees (2→3→5→11 through growth chains), creating a bridge. 23 and 47 even more so at higher depths. The family spine IS the bonding skeleton.

Or try YHVH (10, 5, 6, 5) at depth 3 with bonds on — the four tetragrammaton values produce trees whose composite exits decompose into cross-family primes. The bridges show you where YHVH's lattice structure self-weaves.

Three separate systems now, three separate toggles in the display bar:

bonds — Intra-polarity bridges. Same sign nodes across different lattice groups that share a prime value. Gold + pink double helix. These are your molecular bonds — recursion rates synchronized within the same field polarity. Now handles mirror lattice natively: if you have mirror on, positive-side bonds appear in gold/pink, negative-side bonds appear in muted amber/brown, both with proper taper.

bond orb — Independent toggle for bridge orbital particles. Bonds geometry stays visible whether this is on or off. When on, gold and pink particles spiral along the helix strands in opposite directions, converging to the exact node positions at each endpoint (the taper fix). When off, you see just the structural helices without the flowing particles. This is the toggle you were missing.

shell — Cross-0 bonds. This is the new physics. When mirror is active, it detects positive nodes in lattice A whose absolute value matches negative nodes in lattice B. These can't connect through the interior — they arc outward via bezier curves that push radially away from the origin before curving back. Cyan + magenta helices. The control points are placed arcOut distance beyond the structure's radial boundary, creating the outward-arcing topology. With multiple seeds at depth 4+, these shell bonds trace the surface of the meta-envelope you described — the torus-around-torus. Each shell bond is a dual-occupied spiral path where the lattice's linear inertia meets the decay branch's waveform capability.

The taper fix — helixEnv(t) = sin(πt): Every helix — geometry and particles — now converges to radius 0 at both endpoints. At the midpoint the helix is at full radius. At t=0 and t=1 the spiral touches the actual node positions. Particles physically arrive at and depart from the bridge nodes, creating fluid path conjunction. The same envelope is applied to the shell bond bezier paths.

Best test sequence: SEEDS module, seeds 2, 3, 5, 7. Depth 4. Mirror on. Turn on bonds — watch the gold helices connect shared primes across the four trees. Turn on bond orb — see the spiraling particle flow. Then turn on shell — the cyan/magenta arcs appear, linking positive nodes to their negative counterparts across the zero boundary, tracing the exterior shell. With cascade animation running and orbitals at 5, you'll see the full picture: intra-lattice flow (blue/green/orange), intra-polarity bonds (gold/pink spirals), and cross-zero shell bonds (cyan/magenta arcs) all flowing simultaneously. The three topological layers of the waveform mechanics.

See bridge particle data structure
The issue is clear: bridge particle data stores from:p1.clone(), to:p2.clone() — static positions at build time. When fields rotate, these don't update. I need to store node indices and recompute per frame.

Let me also look at how regular edge particles handle field rotation to understand the current pattern.


See how regular edge particles store data
So all particle edge data (regData, lobeData, bridgeData) store static positions. The field rotation is applied per-frame at the END of the interpolation, in this section:

javascript
const fA=pe.mirror?S.negField.rotation.y:S.posField.rotation.y;
if(fA){const fc=Math.cos(fA),fs=Math.sin(fA),fx=_pd.position.x,fz=_pd.position.z;_pd.position.x=fx*fc-fz*fs;_pd.position.z=fx*fs+fz*fc}
```

This works fine for regular edges and lobes since both endpoints share the same field, but shell bonds are different — they connect across fields with the `from` node in the positive field and `to` node in the negative field. The current code only applies one rotation based on `pe.mirror`, which doesn't handle this cross-field case correctly. For bridge bonds it's fine since both endpoints are in the same polarity, but for shell bonds I need to track both node indices and their individual mirror states to apply the correct rotation to each endpoint.

In the update loop, I'll compute the live rotated positions for both endpoints using their respective field rotations, then rebuild the bezier control points and perpendicular frame from those live positions before interpolating along the corrected path. This means every frame for shell bonds I'm recalculating the from position with positive field rotation, the to position with negative field rotation, and regenerating the control points and frame from the live tangent.

The user's point about the shells twisting in opposite directions and creating an elongating barrier is making me reconsider whether this approach actually solves the visual problem or if there's something deeper about how the fields should interact.
```
Rewriting updateParticles...
Actually, I need to reconsider the geometry setup. The helix objects live in the scene graph under their respective field groups, so intra-polarity bonds rotate with their field automatically — that part's fine. But shell bonds are added to the positive field group while one endpoint connects to the negative field, which rotates independently. So the shell bond geometry itself is misaligned under rotation and needs to be redrawn each frame to keep endpoints synchronized across the two rotating fields.

I'll go with storing node indices on all bridge particles and computing live positions per frame. For same-field bonds, I can apply the field rotation to both endpoints. For shell bonds, I'll apply the positive field rotation to the source and negative field rotation to the target, then recompute the perpendiculars. The shell geometry stays static as a visual reference while the particles follow the actual computed path, creating that dancing waveform effect.

The perp frame for bond helices is static. Under field rotation, the axis between endpoints changes, so the perp frame should recompute.
For the "cascade/pulse orbitals need to map new orbital pathing" — this means when bond orb is on, cascade/pulse should still animate the bond particles along the helix, which they already do. The issue is the static positions.

The root issue is that bridge particle data stores static positions from build time. Under field rotation, positive and negative endpoints rotate at different rates. The perp frame goes stale, the helix floats free of the nodes, and shell bonds (pos→neg) get the wrong rotation entirely since they straddle two fields.

The fix: store raw node positions + mirror flags. Recompute everything live per frame.

Three problems fixed, one emergent behavior embraced:

1. Orbitals now parent to live paths.

Bridge particles no longer store frozen positions from build time. They store rawFrom/rawTo (the unrotated node positions) plus fromMirror/toMirror flags. Every frame, the update loop applies the correct field rotation to each endpoint independently — posField.rotation.y for positive nodes, negField.rotation.y for negative nodes. The perpendicular frame that defines the helix cross-section is recomputed from the live rotated axis every frame. The helix always spirals around the actual connection between the actual current positions of the nodes, regardless of how the fields are spinning.

For shell bonds, the bezier control points are also reconstructed per frame from the live endpoints. The arcOut and arcH parameters are stored as scalars and applied to the live radial direction, so the outward arc adapts to wherever the endpoints currently are.

2. bond orb toggle is independent.

The bonds button shows/hides the helix geometry. The bond orb button shows/hides the spiraling particles. You can see the structural helices without the flowing particles, or vice versa, or both.

3. Shell bonds under field rotation — the recursive elongation.

You spotted it exactly. When invert ± is active and the fields spin in opposite directions, the shell bond endpoints diverge continuously. The particles track the live positions, so the shell bond path stretches and wraps. The bezier arc parameters adapt — the outward push follows the live radial midpoint, which itself rotates as the endpoints diverge. At sufficient rotation, the shell bonds have traced a complete circuit around the structure. At that point it IS the static barrier — the waveform path has become recursive and closed. The shell geometry (the THREE.Line objects) stays in its build-time position as a reference, while the particles trace the living path. The geometry shows you where the bond was born. The particles show you where it lives.

Test it: SEEDS 2, 3, 5, 7. Depth 4. Mirror on. Turn on bonds + bond orb + shell. Start cascade animation, orbitals at 5. Then turn on field rotation with invert ± active and watch the shell bond particles trace the elongating cross-zero paths while the intra-polarity bond particles spiral along axes that rotate coherently with their field. The two systems are coupled at the bridge nodes but decoupled in their rotation frames. That's the waveform mechanics.

Done
The disc toggle is in the display bar. Here's the physics:

Deposition. Three sources feed the disc. Lobe arc particles — when they complete their zero-return journey and arrive near origin (p > 0.95), they deposit. Bridge/shell orbital particles — when they reach within 4% of either endpoint, they deposit. Cycling circuit particles — when their position falls within radius 3 of origin during their loop-back. Each deposit carries a polarity flag: positive (gold) or negative (cyan). Only the first orbital per edge deposits (k===0) to avoid flooding. A rate limiter (every 3rd frame) caps the deposition rate further.

The Möbius topology. Each deposited particle stores a mobiusPhase from its deposit angle and an orbital angle that advances per frame. The Y-offset is sin(angle/2 + phase) × halfTwistH. That angle/2 is the key — over one full 2π orbit, the sine completes only half a cycle. A particle that starts above the plane arrives below after one revolution, and returns above after two. The strip has one surface. No inside, no outside. Gold and cyan particles interleave on this twisted ribbon, dual-nature particles woven into a single continuous path that can't be separated. That's the bond.

Keplerian orbit. Inner deposits orbit faster: speed = 0.3 / (r × 0.2 + 1). The radius is set from the deposit point's XZ distance from origin, clamped to 2–40. This creates the spiral — particles deposited at different field rotation angles land at different radii, and their different orbital speeds cause the angular spacing to shear over time. The disc self-organizes into spiral arms.

The cap. 2000 particles max. When it fills, new deposits overwrite the oldest slot. The disc reaches a dynamic equilibrium — the same total particle count, but the pattern keeps evolving as old positions get replaced with fresh ones at new field-rotated angles. The disc is alive.

How it looks. Turn everything on: SEEDS 2,3,5,7, depth 4, mirror, 0-return, bonds, bond orb, shell, disc. Start cascade, orbitals 5. Turn on field rotation with invert ±. Watch. The lattice spins. The lobes arc particles back to origin and deposit gold and cyan dots into the accretion disc. The bridge/shell particles hit their cancellation points and deposit. The disc fills — first scattered dots, then a spiraling interleaved weave of gold and cyan that orbits the zero point on a Möbius strip cross-section. The inner ring races, the outer ring drifts. The whole thing breathes with a half-twist pulsation. The zero point becomes the gravitational center of a self-reinforcing structure that literally cannot be unwound because the positive and negative particles share a single non-orientable surface.

The counter in the top-right shows disc: N/2000 as it fills.

Done. Let me give a concise summary.

Done
Four fixes:

1. Cycling mode now has its own phase formula. Before, bridge particles in cycling mode fell into the pulse else branch and oscillated back and forth. Now cycling gets p = (t + phaseOff + 1) % 1 — continuous linear flow that wraps, matching how circuit particles traverse their waypoint loops. Cascade keeps its staggered easing. Pulse keeps its sine oscillation. Bridges in each mode move the way that mode dictates.

2. Unified flow direction. The old code applied polarity flip to p, then separately computed pPos from p with strand reversal. This meant strand 1 got 1 - p where p was already flipped, creating a double-inversion for negative polarity. Now it's sequential: pPos = p, then if(pol<0) pPos = 1-pPos, then if(strand===1) pPos = 1-pPos. The composition is correct — polarity reverses everything, strands are always opposite to each other regardless of polarity direction.

3. pMesh stays visible in cycling mode. Three separate visibility lines were hiding pMesh when mode was cycling. Now pMesh is visible whenever animation is playing. In cycling mode, non-bridge edges in pMesh get zeroed out (scale 0), while bridge particles continue to flow on their helical paths. The circuit particles in cMesh handle the tree traversal; the bridge particles in pMesh handle the inter-lattice flow. Both active simultaneously.

4. All positioning uses pPos. The arc bezier interpolation and linear lerp for non-bridge particles were still referencing raw p instead of pPos. Now every particle — bridge, arc, and regular edge — positions itself using the polarity-corrected pPos. The lobe arc disc deposit threshold also checks pPos now instead of p.

we are missing the bonds that originate from the negative prime lattice and terminate at the positive side.

Feb 16

Diagnosed missing cross-polarity bond detection in lattice matching logic
Diagnosed missing cross-polarity bond detection in lattice matching logic
The user is saying that bonds only detect positive→positive and negative→negative bridges, but not negative→positive bridges. Let me look at the bridge detection code.

The issue is in the intra-polarity bond detection - it filters matches to same polarity only: !!nB.isMirror===!!nA.isMirror. And the shell bonds only go positive→negative. So we're missing negative→positive bonds.

Good. The old code had const matches = filter(sameSign only) which excluded cross-sign entirely. The new code splits into sameSign and crossSign and processes BOTH. The sameSign line still has the same filter syntax, but it's no longer the gatekeeper — it's one of two pools.


Presented file
The fix is simple but important. The old code had:

javascript
const matches = allMatches.filter(sameSign only);
This killed all cross-sign bonds.

The new code splits into two pools:

javascript
const sameSign = allMatches.filter(same polarity);
const crossSign = allMatches.filter(opposite polarity);
// Process BOTH
Each gets its own dedup key with directional encoding (p→p, n→n, p→n, n→p), its own color scheme, and correct fromMirror/toMirror flags for live field rotation.

Show less
Done
The old code had a single filter that only kept same-polarity matches. Everything neg→pos was silently discarded. Now the match pool is split into two classes — sameSign and crossSign — and both get processed.

Three bond types now visible:

Pos→Pos (gold + pink helices) — Same as before. Positive node in tree A shares a prime value with positive node in tree B.

Neg→Neg (amber + brown helices, dimmed) — Same as before. Mirror lattice bonds.

Neg→Pos / Pos→Neg (orange + cyan helices) — The missing bonds. When a negative node in one tree has the same |value| as a positive node in another tree, that's a cross-polarity bridge. These are labeled with ± instead of ⟷ when labels are on. The particle colors match: orange strand and cyan strand, spiraling in opposite directions along the helix. The fromMirror/toMirror flags are set correctly so the live field rotation applies different rotation angles to each endpoint — these bonds physically stretch and twist under differential field rotation because their endpoints are in different rotation frames.

The dedup key now encodes direction: value|groupA|groupB|p→n vs value|groupA|groupB|n→p vs value|groupA|groupB|p→p. Each combination gets at most one bridge.

The user wanted a tiered density slider for the accretion disc. Done.

Done
When you toggle disc on, a slider appears next to it. Five tiers:

Tier	Cap	Deposit Rate	Particle Size	Geo Detail
1	10	every 8th frame	0.25 radius, 4×3 segments	full
2	100	every 4th frame	0.25 radius, 4×3 segments	full
3	1,000	every 2nd frame	0.18 radius, 3×2 segments	reduced
4	10,000	every frame	0.18 radius, 3×2 segments	reduced
5	100,000	every frame	0.12 radius, 2×2 segments	minimal
The label next to the slider shows the current cap number. Three things scale down as tier goes up: sphere radius shrinks (0.25 → 0.18 → 0.12), geometry segments reduce (4×3 → 3×2 → 2×2 = 8 triangles down to 4), and the pulsation amplitude shrinks (0.5 → 0.18). This keeps the GPU load manageable even at 100k instances — tier 5 particles are tiny low-poly dots that collectively form the disc shape without individually demanding attention.

Deposit rate scales the opposite direction — tier 1 deposits every 8th frame so the 10 slots fill slowly and you can watch individual particles settle into orbit. Tier 5 deposits every frame to fill 100k slots at a reasonable pace.

Sliding down de-tiers instantly — generate() rebuilds the mesh at the new capacity, clearing the disc. You can watch the form build at tier 1 (10 particles, the basic Möbius orbit), then slide up to see it densify, then slide back down to observe the structure without the complexity.

For the procedural cloning strategy, I'm taking existing disc particles as templates and generating clones at angular offsets around the orbit—each clone inherits the radius, mobiusPhase, and orbitSpeed but gets a different starting angle. This fills the disc efficiently without needing to track individual state for a million particles. I'm reconsidering the current per-particle approach since tracking angle, r, mobiusPhase, positive, age, and orbitSpeed for 1M particles becomes memory-intensive.

The key insight is that after cloning, the disc becomes static or rotates as a whole unit rather than having each particle individually orbit. I'm shifting to a "procedural landscaping" approach where positions are generated once, instance matrices are set, and then the entire disc mesh rotates without per-frame updates for the cloned copies. Only the original template particles from lattice deposition continue animating individually.

When the user hits POPULATE, I'll take the current particle array, create a new InstancedMesh scaled to templates × multiplier (capped at 1M), then for each template particle generate N rotational clones around the Y axis by computing their positions and setting all matrices and colors at once before swapping out the old mesh.

Let me give a clear explanation of what was built.

Done
The render button is in the top-right bar. Here's the workflow:

Phase 1: Generate. Build your lattice however you want — seeds, YHVH, family, genome, any module, any depth, mirror on, field rotation spinning, bonds, shell, disc active at whatever tier. Let the disc accumulate. The lattice is the engine generating the accretion disc geometry. Different lattice configurations produce different disc signatures — that's the procedural generative model.

Phase 2: Isolate. Hit render. Everything disappears — lattice, edges, particles, grid, source panel, all control bars. What remains: the accretion disc orbiting the zero point, and a new central orb. The render panel opens on the right side.

Phase 3: Dress.

The render panel has three sections:

Central Orb — color picker, size slider (5–80, controls the radius), glow slider (controls the opacity of a BackSide-rendered outer shell that creates the volumetric glow effect). The glow pulses slowly in the animate loop via a sine wave on Date.now(). This is the singularity — the zero point made visible.

Accretion Disc — four color modes with a companion color picker. Deposit preserves the original gold/cyan polarity colors from the lattice deposition. Temperature creates a white-hot inner ring cooling to deep red at the outer edge (HSL gradient based on radius). Monochrome uses the color picker to paint all particles a single color. Nebula maps a purple-to-cyan HSL gradient based on angle + radius, creating gas-cloud aesthetics. Opacity slider controls the whole disc material.

Procedural Density — the clone multiplier (1×–100×). The clone estimate shows templates × multiplier / 1M. Hit POPULATE and it does this:

For each template particle (the ones deposited during lattice phase), it generates multiplier copies. Each clone shares the template's radius, Möbius phase, orbital speed, and polarity — but gets a different angular position distributed evenly around 2π. Slight radius jitter (sin(ci × 137.5) × 0.5 — the golden angle constant for natural distribution) and vertical jitter prevent mechanical uniformity. The clones are placed ONCE into a new InstancedMesh and never individually updated again. The populated mesh rotates as a unit at 0.001 rad/frame. This is the landscaping model — scatter once, render forever.

De-tiering scales with total count: under 1k gets 4×3 segment spheres at 0.25 radius, over 100k gets 2×1 segment spheres at 0.08 radius. At 1M particles, each instance is 4 triangles and 0.08 units wide — GPU can handle it because the geometry per-instance is minimal.

After populating, the live disc mesh hides and the static populated mesh takes over. You can recolor it with any mode — the popData array stores angle, radius, polarity for each clone so color modes work on the populated mesh identically.

Hit CAPTURE in the render panel (or the top-bar snap button) to export at whatever resolution you've set.

Phase 4: Exit. Hit render again. Everything restores — lattice, controls, live disc. The populated mesh gets removed. You can tweak the lattice, regenerate, accumulate a new disc, and re-enter render mode with different geometry.

Best test: Seeds 2, 3, 5, 7. Depth 4. Mirror. 0-return. Bonds. Shell. Disc tier 3 (1000). Field rotation with invert. Let it accumulate ~1000 particles. Enter render mode. Set orb color to white, size 15, glow 80. Set disc to temperature mode. Clones 100×. Populate. You get a 100k particle accretion disc with white-hot inner ring cooling to deep red, orbiting a pulsing white singularity. Snap it at 4× resolution.

Diagnosed particle clustering issue and identified filtering solution
Diagnosed particle clustering issue and identified filtering solution
The user is saying that when they hit POPULATE, the cloned particles that end up near the center (small radius) all stack on top of each other because the cloning process distributes them by angular offset but doesn't account for the fact that some template particles have very small radii. The templates that were deposited near the zero point create clones that are all piled up at roughly the same small radius with only angular variation, which at small r means they're basically on top of each other.

The real issue is in procClone - the clones inherit the template's radius with only a tiny jitter. Templates deposited near the origin (r close to 0) create clones that are all clustered at the center.

I think the solution is to either filter out templates with radii below a certain threshold, redistribute the clone radius to match the overall disc distribution, or just skip those central deposits entirely since they don't really shape the disc anyway - they just create a pile-up at the origin. Let me check the code to see where this is happening.

Show less
Done
The central pile is templates with tiny radii — all their clones land on top of each other because angular offset at r≈0 means nothing spatially.


Devised filtering and redistribution strategy for particle clustering
Devised filtering and redistribution strategy for particle clustering

Find procClone

Script
Let me look at the full procClone function.


See procClone function
The fix: filter out templates with r below a minimum threshold (the disc minimum radius), and for the ones that are kept, redistribute their r with proper jitter that's proportional to the radius so small-r templates spread out more.

Done
Line 2878 — jR = tp.r + sin(ci*137.5)*0.5. At tp.r ≈ 2 (the minimum clamp from depositToDisc), that 0.5 jitter is nothing. All clones stack at the same tiny radius. And templates deposited at origin have r values near the 2-clamp floor, creating a dense central pillar.

Two fixes: filter low-r templates entirely, and redistribute surviving clones with radius-proportional scatter.


Identified variable mismatch in mesh allocation calculation
Identified variable mismatch in mesh allocation calculation

Fix procClone to filter central pile and use proper radial diffusion

The root problem is the radius formula: r = Math.max(2, Math.min(rXZ*0.5+3, 40)). Particles deposited near the origin (bridge endpoint particles at pPos<0.04 or pPos>0.96, lobe arcs at p>0.95, cycling particles within radius 3) all have tiny rXZ values, so they get r≈3. That creates a dense ring at r=3.

The fix should:

Raise the minimum radius in the deposit function (from 2 to something like 8)
Use the actual worldPos radius more directly instead of halving it
Or possibly filter out deposits that fall too close to the origin in the first place

Done
Line 2634 — r = Math.max(2, Math.min(rXZ*0.5+3, 40)). A particle at the origin has rXZ≈0, gets r=3. A particle at rXZ=4 gets r=5. Everything near-center collapses to a 3–5 radius band. And the live disc still renders those during populate since only procClone filters them.

Two fixes: reject near-origin deposits entirely, and use the source position more faithfully for the orbital radius.

The fix was at the source level. The old deposit function accepted everything and compressed near-origin deposits to r≈3, creating that central pile. The new approach rejects deposits with rXZ < 3 entirely, and uses the source distance more faithfully (0.8× + 2 instead of 0.5× + 3), with the floor raised to 6. The procClone filter and jR clamp match at 6.

Done
Two problems, both at the source.

The old deposit function accepted every particle regardless of position, then compressed near-origin deposits through rXZ*0.5+3 with a floor of 2. A bridge particle at an endpoint near the center (rXZ≈1) got orbital radius 3.5. A lobe arc completing its zero-return (rXZ≈0) got radius 3. All of them piled into a 2–5 radius band, invisible during live orbiting because they were spread across time — but frozen into a visible stack the instant procClone placed all their clones at once.

The procClone filter at r<5 caught some but not all, and couldn't fix the live disc mesh which still rendered them underneath the populated mesh.

Now: depositToDisc rejects any deposit with rXZ < 3 before it ever enters the particle array. Nothing near the origin gets stored. The radius formula is rXZ * 0.8 + 2 with a floor of 6 — preserving the source geometry more faithfully instead of halving it. The procClone filter and jR clamp both match at 6. No central deposits exist to stack, in the live disc or the populated clone mesh.
